perturbation.original_text: “Confidential Information ” shall mean information including, but not limited to, the existence of the discussions between the Company and Recipient or any third-party regarding future business relationships and information regarding the Company’s products, services, product designs, plans, roadmaps, prices, costs, trade secrets, inventions, intellectual property, development plans, tooling, equipment, import or export controls, license arrangements or agreements, methods, techniques, proprietary processes and know-how, programs, schematics, software, data, customer lists, financial information, inside information (including information regarding financial performance, earnings, existing products, existing techniques, new products, new techniques and business strategies), personnel information (including, without limitation, skills and compensation), product development information, client development information, information regarding possible acquisitions or sales of businesses or facilities sales, marketing plans, business opportunities, research and development activities, pre-release products, information posted on the Company’s web site (to the extent that such information is not publicly accessible), or any other information which the Recipient knows or reasonably should know is confidential, proprietary or trade secret information of the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: “Confidential Information ” shall mean information including only the information explicitly marked as "Confidential" in writing by the Company. No other information, regardless of its nature, shall be considered Confidential Information under this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: The original definition of "Confidential Information" is broad. The modified text narrows the definition substantially, contradicting the common understanding and legal definition of what constitutes a trade secret or confidential information.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text severely limits the scope of 'Confidential Information,' potentially excluding information that would otherwise be protected as a trade secret under the Washington Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA). RCW 19.108.010(4) defines 'trade secret' more broadly than information 'explicitly marked as "Confidential" in writing.'
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: RCW 19.108.010: Definitions. Print RCWs > Title 19 > Chapter 19.108 > Section 19.108.010 Beginning of Chapter << 19.108.010 >> 19.108.020 PDF RCW 19.108.010 Definitions. Unless the context clearly requires otherwise, the definitions set forth in this section apply throughout this chapter. (1) "Improper means" includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means; (2) "Misappropriation" means: (a) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (b) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (i) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (ii) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was (A) derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it, (B) acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use, or (C) derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Before a material change of his or her position, knew or had reason to know that it was a trade secret and that knowledge of it had been acquired by accident or mistake. (3) "Person" means a natural person, corporation, business trust, estate, trust, partnership, association, joint venture, government, governmental subdivision or agency, or any other legal or commercial entity. (4) "Trade secret" means information, including a formula, pattern, compilation, program, device, method, technique, or process that: (a) Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable by proper means by, other persons who can obtain economic value from its disclosure or use; and (b) Is the subject

perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the definition of "trade secret" as outlined in Washington's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (UTSA), RCW 19.108.010(4). The original text broadly defines confidential information, while the modified text restricts it to only information explicitly marked as "Confidential" in writing. RCW 19.108.010(4)(a) states that a 'trade secret' includes information that 'Derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known...' This definition does not require the information to be explicitly marked as confidential, only that it derives economic value from being secret. The modified text's requirement for explicit marking would exclude information that meets the statutory definition of a trade secret but is not explicitly labeled, creating a legal contradiction. The snippet's broad definition makes it clear that the change is not just a minor adjustment, but a direct narrowing of the scope of confidential information, undermining the protections afforded by the UTSA.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient agrees to use all reasonable precautions, no less than Recipient’s treatment of its own Confidential Information of a similar nature, to prevent the unauthorized disclosure of the Company’s Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient agrees to use precautions that it deems necessary, but is not responsible for ensuring the confidentiality of information.
perturbation.explanation: This modification significantly weakens the obligation to protect confidential information. By stating that the Recipient is not responsible for ensuring confidentiality, it contradicts the implied duty of care typically associated with handling confidential information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Duty of Care under Washington State Law - Implied Contractual Obligations
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a statute, this modification violates the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing present in every contract under common law, as adopted in Washington state. By removing the responsibility for confidentiality, the agreement contradicts the reasonable expectations of the parties and undermines the purpose of the NDA.

perturbation.justification: The original text requires the recipient to use "all reasonable precautions" to protect confidential information. The changed text states the recipient "is not responsible for ensuring the confidentiality of information." This directly contradicts the implied duty of care in handling confidential information, effectively nullifying the obligation to protect it. The Law Explanation supports this by stating the modification violates the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing present in every contract, undermining the purpose of the NDA. While there are no scraped snippets to support this, the LLM's explanation describes a clear contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed under the laws of the State of Washington. Recipient irrevocably consents and submits to the exclusive and mandatory jurisdiction of the state courts sitting in Skagit County, Washington or the U.S. District Court for the Western District of Washington, and agrees that any action, suit or proceeding in connection with this Agreement shall be brought only in such courts to the exclusion of all other courts, other than actions to enforce judgments or orders entered in said courts.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed under the laws of the State of Delaware, regardless of the location of either party. Any dispute arising from this agreement will be settled through binding arbitration in the state of Nevada.
perturbation.explanation: The change of governing law and venue from Washington to Delaware and Nevada introduces jurisdictional uncertainty and deviates from standard practices. The original text specifies Washington law and courts, while the modified text chooses Delaware law and mandatory arbitration in Nevada.
perturbation.law_explanation: While parties can agree to a forum selection clause, the change to Delaware law and arbitration in Nevada might be challenged if it's deemed unreasonable or lacks a substantial relationship to the parties or the transaction. 28 U.S. Code § 1404 allows for a change of venue for the convenience of parties and witnesses, and in the interest of justice. The modified clause might conflict with this principle if the connection to Delaware and Nevada is tenuous.

(b) Upon motion, consent or stipulation of all parties, any action, suit or proceeding of a civil nature or any motion or hearing thereof, may be transferred, in t
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces a contradiction by changing the governing law and venue. The original text specified Washington law and courts, while the changed text designates Delaware law and mandatory arbitration in Nevada. This shift introduces jurisdictional uncertainty and potentially violates the principle of venue convenience, as described in 28 U.S.C. § 1404, which states that a court may transfer a civil action for the convenience of parties and witnesses, in the interest of justice, to another district where it might have been brought. The modified clause might conflict with this principle if the connection to Delaware and Nevada is tenuous.

perturbation.original_text: Recipient may disclose the Company’s Confidential Information if and only to the extent that a disclosure is required by applicable law, provided that Recipient uses reasonable efforts to limit the disclosure by means of a protective order or a request for Confidential/Attorneys’ Eyes Only treatment that permits the Company a reasonable opportunity to review the proposed disclosure before it is made, and to interpose its own objection to the disclosure and, if appropriate, bring a motion for a protective order.
perturbation.changed_text: Recipient may disclose the Company’s Confidential Information if and only to the extent that a disclosure is required by applicable law and is not required to notify the Company.
perturbation.explanation: This removes the requirement for the Recipient to seek a protective order or give the Company a chance to object before disclosing information required by law. This limits the Company's ability to protect its confidential information even when disclosure is legally mandated, potentially harming its business interests.
perturbation.law_explanation: This modification undermines the protections afforded by Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 26(c), which allows a party to seek a protective order to protect confidential information from being disclosed during discovery or other legal proceedings. By removing the obligation to notify the Company and seek a protective order, the agreement hinders the Company's ability to invoke Rule 26(c) and safeguard its confidential information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 26. Duty to Disclose; General Provisions Governing Discovery (a) Required Disclosures. (1) Initial Disclosure. (A) In General. Except as exempted by Rule 26(a)(1)(B) or as otherwise stipulated or ordered by the court, a party must, without awaiting a discovery request, provide to the other parties: (i) the name and, if known, the address and telephone number of each individual likely to have discoverable information—along with the subjects of that information—that the disclosing party may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (ii) a copy—or a description by category and location—of all documents, electronically stored information, and tangible things that the disclosing party has in its possession, custody, or control and may use to support its claims or defenses, unless the use would be solely for impeachment; (iii) a computation of each category of damages claimed by the disclosing party—who must also make available for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 the documents or other evidentiary material, unless privileged or protected from disclosure, on which each computation is based, including materials bearing on the nature and extent of injuries suffered; and (iv) for inspection and copying as under Rule 34 , any insurance agreement under which an insurance business may be liable to satisfy all or part of a possible judgment in the action or to indemnify or reimburse for payments made to satisfy the judgment. (B) Proceedings Exempt from Initial Disclosure. The following proceedings are exempt from initial disclosure: (i) an action for review on an administrative record; (ii) a forfeiture action in rem arising from a federal statute; (iii) a petition for habeas corpus or any other proceeding to challenge a criminal convictio
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure Skip to main content Official websites use .gov A .gov website belongs to an official government organization in the United States. Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modification removes the requirement for the Recipient to notify the Company and seek a protective order before disclosing confidential information required by law. This directly impacts the Company's ability to protect its confidential information, which could be achieved through measures like seeking a protective order under Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 26(c). The 'Law Explanation' highlights this point. Scraped Law Snippet 1 mentions Rule 26 and its provisions for discovery, implying the possibility of protecting confidential information during legal proceedings, which the original contract aimed to facilitate, but the changed contract prevents.
